'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Kuzumi-jinja}} [[JA:葛見神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11621015}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Kuzumi Shrine |画像 = [[File:Kuzumi-jinja 20100612.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|34|57|50.6|N|139|05|56|E}}}} |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}{{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163}}1-16-40 Babachō | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 57 | 緯度秒 = 50.6 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 5 | 経度秒 = 56 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | ISO = JP-22 | 地図 = Japan Shizuoka | ラベル位置 = left |祭神 = Kuzumi deity |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |別名 = Oka no Gōsha |札所等 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = Oka Kagura / Torisashi Odori ({{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}) }} '''Kuzumi Shrine''' (くずみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}. It is identified with "Kuzumi (Kutsumi) Shrine" of Tagata District, {{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|WD=Q860928}}, in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} list; its former rank was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. It stands at the northern foot of a small hill about 30 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573}} high in the southern urban area of Itō. In the densely wooded precinct rises a giant {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=camphor tree|1=UNKNOWN}} designated a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}}. === Shrine name === A {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} of the present honden shows that in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1697|Q7702|WD=Q7702}}) it was called "Kuzumi Taisha." Earlier names include "Kusumi Shrine" and "Kugo Taisha," and after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}} it was renamed to the current form'Shizuoka-ken Jinja-shi,' Shizuoka-ken Chōdo Kenkyūkai, 1941.. In this case, the shrine name is derived from local place names (ancient {{ill|lt=Kusumi-gō (Itō)|Q721163|WD=Q721163|lt=Kusumi-gō}}, medieval Kusumi / Kuzumi / Nanmi-shō). It is thought the old reading "kutsumi" voiced to "kuzumi" and became current "kuzumi"'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.'. The site belonged to Oka village until early modern times, and up to {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 47 ({{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476}}) the address was Ito-shi Oka Aza Miyanoue, so it is still commonly called "Oka no Gōsha." === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is '''Kuzumi deity''', with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}) in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883}}. The principal deity is variously said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611}} or a related clan deity, or medicinal deities such as {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q3275687}}, but none is certain. Since sites in this area date back to the {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824}}, it is considered to have been inhabited early and the deity likely revered from old times as the tutelary deity of the area. The aiden deity was invited later by the {{ill|lt=Itō clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280}}; because their ancestor {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketaka|Q24860857|WD=Q24860857|lt=Kudō Suketaka (Kudō Ietsugu / Itō Ietsugu)}} revered Inari, it is thought Inari was merged here'Nihon no Kamigami.'. The Itō lineage is said to have followed Suketaka in revering Inari, and among branches dispersed to various regions there were many cases of inviting Inari at their new settlements. === Origin and history === From ancient times, rites were likely performed to the sacred grove where divine spirit dwelled, still flourishing in the precinct today. The "Kuzumi Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is considered this shrineThere is also a theory identifying it with {{ill|lt=Yumae Shrine|Q11563474|WD=Q11563474}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364}} (e.g., 'Nihon Chiri Shiryō')., but its name does not appear in the '{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkaichō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087}},' a {{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490}}. In the medieval period it received reverence and protection from the Itō clan based in this area. Itō Suketaka repaired the shrine and founded nearby Tōrin-ji as a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} to manage it'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.' Tōrin-ji's mountain name is "Inari-zan," thought to derive from enshrining Inari ('Nihon no Kamigami'). There is also a tradition that it was founded by Sukechika (Suketaka's grandson) to pray for the soul of his son {{ill|lt=Kawazu Sukeyasu|Q11553998|WD=Q11553998|lt=Sukeyasu}}, slain by {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketsune|Q11479615|WD=Q11479615}}, his in-law cousin (same source). See also "{{ill|lt=The Soga Brothers' Revenge|Q10324810|WD=Q10324810}}.". Though Itō power declined after death of Suketaka's grandson {{ill|lt=Itō Sukechika|Q11379553|WD=Q11379553|lt=Sukechika}}, descendants remaining nearby appear to have continued faith in the shrine. According to shrine munafuda, in {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 15 ({{ill|lt=1610|Q6765|WD=Q6765}}), {{ill|lt=Itō Masayo|Q9385264|WD=Q9385264|lt=Itō Masa(yo)yo}} rebuilt after fire (though this is said to refer to the stone hokora for Inari/Hōsō deity under the great camphor noted belowAkiyama Akira, 'Tōshū Shikō,' written in {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} 12 (1800).). Until {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, local rulers periodically donated rice offerings. In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) April, it was ranked gōsha. The shrine was also treated as sacred by nearby residents. It is said funeral processions were strictly forbidden from passing the road in front of the shrine; even though it was the main road to Tōrin-ji, funeral routes to that temple used detours. === Rituals === === Shinto rites === Until early modern times, festival day was September 19; after calendar reform it changed to October 19, and now the annual festival is October 15. At the festival, "Oka Kagura," {{ill|lt=Sanbasō|Q11356796|WD=Q11356796}}, and Torisashi dance are dedicated at the precinct chōya building. "Oka Kagura" was originally a {{ill|lt=lion dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866}} performed on the first horse day of FebruaryTateo Hamano, 'Itō-shi,' written in {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era}} (mid-19th century)., thought to be a rite related to Inari. After a period of interruption, it was revived in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 59 and has been dedicated at the annual festival. Torisashi dance is also dedicated at other city shrines; each takes motifs from '{{ill|lt=Soga Monogatari|Q217886|WD=Q217886}}' and depicts the Soga brothers, disguised as bird catchers, avenging their father. Sanbasō is also widely distributed as ritual performance across the {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822}}. === Priests === In early modern times, Tōrin-ji managed the shrine as bettōThe temple was a Shugendō temple of the {{ill|lt=Tōzan-ha|Q11487826|WD=Q11487826}} since the {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853}} era (first half of 16th c.); now {{ill|lt=Sōtō school|Q749085|WD=Q749085}} ('Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku')., but at the start of Meiji, with {{ill|lt=shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, its monk returned to lay status, took the surname Asahi, and the Asahi family has continued the office. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Kuzumi-jinja no Ōkusu 20100612.jpg|thumb|150px|Hōsō deity and giant camphor]] The honden is a 2-ken-square {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} structure protected by a covering building ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ooiya|1=UNKNOWN}}), rebuilt in Genroku 10. Its transom has carvings of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=foxes|1=UNKNOWN}} and other motifs. The covering structure dates from the {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō period}} (early 19th c.). The haiden is a steel-reinforced concrete {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575}} tiled building, 3 bays by 2 bays, built in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 40. === Precinct shrines === Kumano Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, Mishima Shrine, Hachiman Shrine (merging Shinmei and Kasuga shrines), and Hōsō deity shrine. Hōsō deity is enshrined in a stone hokora under the giant camphor natural monument; because some gazetteers call it "Inari," it is thought Inari was worshiped here as {{ill|lt=hōsō deity|Q7543689|WD=Q7543689}}. === Cultural property === *{{ill|lt=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine|Q11621016|WD=Q11621016}} - a giant camphor north (left) of the haiden, trunk circumference about 15 m at chest height. Said to be over 1,000 years oldAlso described as reaching 2,000 years as of {{ill|lt=1987|Q2422|WD=Q2422}} in 'Nihon no Kamigami'.; designated national natural monument on February 28, {{ill|lt=1933|Q18726|WD=Q18726}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 8). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, Kōgakkan University Press, 1981 * Tanigawa Ken'ichi (ed.), 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places' vol.10 Tōkai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くすみしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧郷社]] [[Category:伊東市の歴史]] [[Category:伊東市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Kuzumi-jinja}} [[JA:葛見神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11621015}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Kuzumi Shrine |画像 = [[File:Kuzumi-jinja 20100612.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|34|57|50.6|N|139|05|56|E}}}} |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}{{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163}}1-16-40 Babachō | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 57 | 緯度秒 = 50.6 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 5 | 経度秒 = 56 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | ISO = JP-22 | 地図 = Japan Shizuoka | ラベル位置 = left |祭神 = Kuzumi deity |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |別名 = Oka no Gōsha |札所等 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = Oka Kagura / Torisashi Odori ({{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}) }} '''Kuzumi Shrine''' (くずみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}. It is identified with "Kuzumi (Kutsumi) Shrine" of Tagata District, {{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|WD=Q860928}}, in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} list; its former rank was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. It stands at the northern foot of a small hill about 30 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573}} high in the southern urban area of Itō. In the densely wooded precinct rises a giant {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=camphor tree|1=UNKNOWN}} designated a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}}. === Shrine name === A {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} of the present honden shows that in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1697|Q7702|WD=Q7702}}) it was called "Kuzumi Taisha." Earlier names include "Kusumi Shrine" and "Kugo Taisha," and after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}} it was renamed to the current form'Shizuoka-ken Jinja-shi,' Shizuoka-ken Chōdo Kenkyūkai, 1941.. In this case, the shrine name is derived from local place names (ancient {{ill|lt=Kusumi-gō (Itō)|Q721163|WD=Q721163|lt=Kusumi-gō}}, medieval Kusumi / Kuzumi / Nanmi-shō). It is thought the old reading "kutsumi" voiced to "kuzumi" and became current "kuzumi"'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.'. The site belonged to Oka village until early modern times, and up to {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 47 ({{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476}}) the address was Ito-shi Oka Aza Miyanoue, so it is still commonly called "Oka no Gōsha." === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is '''Kuzumi deity''', with {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}) in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883}}. The principal deity is variously said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611}} or a related clan deity, or medicinal deities such as {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q3275687}}, but none is certain. Since sites in this area date back to the {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824}}, it is considered to have been inhabited early and the deity likely revered from old times as the tutelary deity of the area. The aiden deity was invited later by the {{ill|lt=Itō clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280}}; because their ancestor {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketaka|Q24860857|WD=Q24860857|lt=Kudō Suketaka (Kudō Ietsugu / Itō Ietsugu)}} revered Inari, it is thought Inari was merged here'Nihon no Kamigami.'. The Itō lineage is said to have followed Suketaka in revering Inari, and among branches dispersed to various regions there were many cases of inviting Inari at their new settlements. === Origin and history === From ancient times, rites were likely performed to the sacred grove where divine spirit dwelled, still flourishing in the precinct today. The "Kuzumi Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is considered this shrineThere is also a theory identifying it with {{ill|lt=Yumae Shrine|Q11563474|WD=Q11563474}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364}} (e.g., 'Nihon Chiri Shiryō')., but its name does not appear in the '{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkaichō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087}},' a {{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490}}. In the medieval period it received reverence and protection from the Itō clan based in this area. Itō Suketaka repaired the shrine and founded nearby Tōrin-ji as a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} to manage it'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.' Tōrin-ji's mountain name is "Inari-zan," thought to derive from enshrining Inari ('Nihon no Kamigami'). There is also a tradition that it was founded by Sukechika (Suketaka's grandson) to pray for the soul of his son {{ill|lt=Kawazu Sukeyasu|Q11553998|WD=Q11553998|lt=Sukeyasu}}, slain by {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketsune|Q11479615|WD=Q11479615}}, his in-law cousin (same source). See also "{{ill|lt=The Soga Brothers' Revenge|Q10324810|WD=Q10324810}}.". Though Itō power declined after death of Suketaka's grandson {{ill|lt=Itō Sukechika|Q11379553|WD=Q11379553|lt=Sukechika}}, descendants remaining nearby appear to have continued faith in the shrine. According to shrine munafuda, in {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 15 ({{ill|lt=1610|Q6765|WD=Q6765}}), {{ill|lt=Itō Masayo|Q9385264|WD=Q9385264|lt=Itō Masa(yo)yo}} rebuilt after fire (though this is said to refer to the stone hokora for Inari/Hōsō deity under the great camphor noted belowAkiyama Akira, 'Tōshū Shikō,' written in {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} 12 (1800).). Until {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, local rulers periodically donated rice offerings. In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) April, it was ranked gōsha. The shrine was also treated as sacred by nearby residents. It is said funeral processions were strictly forbidden from passing the road in front of the shrine; even though it was the main road to Tōrin-ji, funeral routes to that temple used detours. === Rituals === === Shinto rites === Until early modern times, festival day was September 19; after calendar reform it changed to October 19, and now the annual festival is October 15. At the festival, "Oka Kagura," {{ill|lt=Sanbasō|Q11356796|WD=Q11356796}}, and Torisashi dance are dedicated at the precinct chōya building. "Oka Kagura" was originally a {{ill|lt=lion dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866}} performed on the first horse day of FebruaryTateo Hamano, 'Itō-shi,' written in {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era}} (mid-19th century)., thought to be a rite related to Inari. After a period of interruption, it was revived in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 59 and has been dedicated at the annual festival. Torisashi dance is also dedicated at other city shrines; each takes motifs from '{{ill|lt=Soga Monogatari|Q217886|WD=Q217886}}' and depicts the Soga brothers, disguised as bird catchers, avenging their father. Sanbasō is also widely distributed as ritual performance across the {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822}}. === Priests === In early modern times, Tōrin-ji managed the shrine as bettōThe temple was a Shugendō temple of the {{ill|lt=Tōzan-ha|Q11487826|WD=Q11487826}} since the {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853}} era (first half of 16th c.); now {{ill|lt=Sōtō school|Q749085|WD=Q749085}} ('Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku')., but at the start of Meiji, with {{ill|lt=shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, its monk returned to lay status, took the surname Asahi, and the Asahi family has continued the office. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Kuzumi-jinja no Ōkusu 20100612.jpg|thumb|150px|Hōsō deity and giant camphor]] The honden is a 2-ken-square {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} structure protected by a covering building ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ooiya|1=UNKNOWN}}), rebuilt in Genroku 10. Its transom has carvings of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=foxes|1=UNKNOWN}} and other motifs. The covering structure dates from the {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō period}} (early 19th c.). The haiden is a steel-reinforced concrete {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575}} tiled building, 3 bays by 2 bays, built in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 40. === Precinct shrines === Kumano Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, Mishima Shrine, Hachiman Shrine (merging Shinmei and Kasuga shrines), and Hōsō deity shrine. Hōsō deity is enshrined in a stone hokora under the giant camphor natural monument; because some gazetteers call it "Inari," it is thought Inari was worshiped here as {{ill|lt=hōsō deity|Q7543689|WD=Q7543689}}. === Cultural property === *{{ill|lt=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine|Q11621016|WD=Q11621016}} - a giant camphor north (left) of the haiden, trunk circumference about 15 m at chest height. Said to be over 1,000 years oldAlso described as reaching 2,000 years as of {{ill|lt=1987|Q2422|WD=Q2422}} in 'Nihon no Kamigami'.; designated national natural monument on February 28, {{ill|lt=1933|Q18726|WD=Q18726}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 8). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, Kōgakkan University Press, 1981 * Tanigawa Ken'ichi (ed.), 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places' vol.10 Tōkai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くすみしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧郷社]] [[Category:伊東市の歴史]] [[Category:伊東市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

Tōrin-ji's mountain name is "Inari-zan," thought to derive from enshrining Inari ('Nihon no Kamigami'). There is also a tradition that it was founded by Sukechika (Suketaka's grandson) to pray for the soul of his son {{ill|lt=Kawazu Sukeyasu|Q11553998|WD=Q11553998|lt=Sukeyasu}}, slain by {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketsune|Q11479615|WD=Q11479615}}, his in-law cousin (same source). See also "{{ill|lt=The Soga Brothers' Revenge|Q10324810|WD=Q10324810}}.". Though Itō power declined after death of Suketaka's grandson {{ill|lt=Itō Sukechika|Q11379553|WD=Q11379553|lt=Sukechika}}, descendants remaining nearby appear to have continued faith in the shrine. According to shrine munafuda, in {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 15 ({{ill|lt=1610|Q6765|WD=Q6765}}), {{ill|lt=Itō Masayo|Q9385264|WD=Q9385264|lt=Itō Masa(yo)yo}} rebuilt after fire (though this is said to refer to the stone hokora for Inari/Hōsō deity under the great camphor noted belowAkiyama Akira, 'Tōshū Shikō,' written in {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} 12 (1800).). Until {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, local rulers periodically donated rice offerings. In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) April, it was ranked gōsha. The shrine was also treated as sacred by nearby residents. It is said funeral processions were strictly forbidden from passing the road in front of the shrine; even though it was the main road to Tōrin-ji, funeral routes to that temple used detours. === Rituals === === Shinto rites === Until early modern times, festival day was September 19; after calendar reform it changed to October 19, and now the annual festival is October 15. At the festival, "Oka Kagura," {{ill|lt=Sanbasō|Q11356796|WD=Q11356796}}, and Torisashi dance are dedicated at the precinct chōya building. "Oka Kagura" was originally a {{ill|lt=lion dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866}} performed on the first horse day of FebruaryTateo Hamano, 'Itō-shi,' written in {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era}} (mid-19th century)., thought to be a rite related to Inari. After a period of interruption, it was revived in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 59 and has been dedicated at the annual festival. Torisashi dance is also dedicated at other city shrines; each takes motifs from '{{ill|lt=Soga Monogatari|Q217886|WD=Q217886}}' and depicts the Soga brothers, disguised as bird catchers, avenging their father. Sanbasō is also widely distributed as ritual performance across the {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822}}. === Priests === In early modern times, Tōrin-ji managed the shrine as bettōThe temple was a Shugendō temple of the {{ill|lt=Tōzan-ha|Q11487826|WD=Q11487826}} since the {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853}} era (first half of 16th c.); now {{ill|lt=Sōtō school|Q749085|WD=Q749085}} ('Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku')., but at the start of Meiji, with {{ill|lt=shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, its monk returned to lay status, took the surname Asahi, and the Asahi family has continued the office. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Kuzumi-jinja no Ōkusu 20100612.jpg|thumb|150px|Hōsō deity and giant camphor]] The honden is a 2-ken-square {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} structure protected by a covering building ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ooiya|1=UNKNOWN}}), rebuilt in Genroku 10. Its transom has carvings of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=foxes|1=UNKNOWN}} and other motifs. The covering structure dates from the {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō period}} (early 19th c.). The haiden is a steel-reinforced concrete {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575}} tiled building, 3 bays by 2 bays, built in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 40. === Precinct shrines === Kumano Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, Mishima Shrine, Hachiman Shrine (merging Shinmei and Kasuga shrines), and Hōsō deity shrine. Hōsō deity is enshrined in a stone hokora under the giant camphor natural monument; because some gazetteers call it "Inari," it is thought Inari was worshiped here as {{ill|lt=hōsō deity|Q7543689|WD=Q7543689}}. === Cultural property === *{{ill|lt=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine|Q11621016|WD=Q11621016}} - a giant camphor north (left) of the haiden, trunk circumference about 15 m at chest height. Said to be over 1,000 years oldAlso described as reaching 2,000 years as of {{ill|lt=1987|Q2422|WD=Q2422}} in 'Nihon no Kamigami'.; designated national natural monument on February 28, {{ill|lt=1933|Q18726|WD=Q18726}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 8). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, Kōgakkan University Press, 1981 * Tanigawa Ken'ichi (ed.), 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places' vol.10 Tōkai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くすみしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧郷社]] [[Category:伊東市の歴史]] [[Category:伊東市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|34|57|50.6|N|139|05|56|E}}}} |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}{{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163}}1-16-40 Babachō | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 57 | 緯度秒 = 50.6 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 5 | 経度秒 = 56 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | ISO = JP-22 | 地図 = Japan Shizuoka | ラベル位置 = left |祭神 = Kuzumi deity |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} |別名 = Oka no Gōsha |札所等 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = Oka Kagura / Torisashi Odori ({{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919}}) }} '''Kuzumi Shrine''' (くずみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}. It is identified with "Kuzumi (Kutsumi) Shrine" of Tagata District, {{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|WD=Q860928}}, in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} list; its former rank was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. It stands at the northern foot of a small hill about 30 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573}} high in the southern urban area of Itō. In the densely wooded precinct rises a giant {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=camphor tree|1=UNKNOWN}} designated a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}}. === Shrine name === A {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}} of the present honden shows that in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1697|Q7702|WD=Q7702}}) it was called "Kuzumi Taisha." Earlier names include "Kusumi Shrine" and "Kugo Taisha," and after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}} it was renamed to the current form'Shizuoka-ken Jinja-shi,' Shizuoka-ken Chōdo Kenkyūkai, 1941.. In this case, the shrine name is derived from local place names (ancient {{ill|lt=Kusumi-gō (Itō)|Q721163|WD=Q721163|lt=Kusumi-gō}}, medieval Kusumi / Kuzumi / Nanmi-shō). It is thought the old reading "kutsumi" voiced to "kuzumi" and became current "kuzumi"'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.'. The site belonged to Oka village until early modern times, and up to {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 47 ({{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476}}) the address was Ito-shi Oka Aza Miyanoue, so it is still commonly called "Oka no Gōsha." === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is '''Kuzumi deity''', with {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665}}) in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883}}. The principal deity is variously said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611}} or a related clan deity, or medicinal deities such as {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q3275687}}, but none is certain. Since sites in this area date back to the {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824}}, it is considered to have been inhabited early and the deity likely revered from old times as the tutelary deity of the area. The aiden deity was invited later by the {{ill|lt=Itō clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280}}; because their ancestor {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketaka|Q24860857|WD=Q24860857|lt=Kudō Suketaka (Kudō Ietsugu / Itō Ietsugu)}} revered Inari, it is thought Inari was merged here'Nihon no Kamigami.'. The Itō lineage is said to have followed Suketaka in revering Inari, and among branches dispersed to various regions there were many cases of inviting Inari at their new settlements. === Origin and history === From ancient times, rites were likely performed to the sacred grove where divine spirit dwelled, still flourishing in the precinct today. The "Kuzumi Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}' is considered this shrineThere is also a theory identifying it with {{ill|lt=Yumae Shrine|Q11563474|WD=Q11563474}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364}} (e.g., 'Nihon Chiri Shiryō')., but its name does not appear in the '{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkaichō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087}},' a {{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490}}. In the medieval period it received reverence and protection from the Itō clan based in this area. Itō Suketaka repaired the shrine and founded nearby Tōrin-ji as a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} to manage it'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.' Tōrin-ji's mountain name is "Inari-zan," thought to derive from enshrining Inari ('Nihon no Kamigami'). There is also a tradition that it was founded by Sukechika (Suketaka's grandson) to pray for the soul of his son {{ill|lt=Kawazu Sukeyasu|Q11553998|WD=Q11553998|lt=Sukeyasu}}, slain by {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketsune|Q11479615|WD=Q11479615}}, his in-law cousin (same source). See also "{{ill|lt=The Soga Brothers' Revenge|Q10324810|WD=Q10324810}}.". Though Itō power declined after death of Suketaka's grandson {{ill|lt=Itō Sukechika|Q11379553|WD=Q11379553|lt=Sukechika}}, descendants remaining nearby appear to have continued faith in the shrine. According to shrine munafuda, in {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185}} 15 ({{ill|lt=1610|Q6765|WD=Q6765}}), {{ill|lt=Itō Masayo|Q9385264|WD=Q9385264|lt=Itō Masa(yo)yo}} rebuilt after fire (though this is said to refer to the stone hokora for Inari/Hōsō deity under the great camphor noted belowAkiyama Akira, 'Tōshū Shikō,' written in {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} 12 (1800).). Until {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, local rulers periodically donated rice offerings. In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) April, it was ranked gōsha. The shrine was also treated as sacred by nearby residents. It is said funeral processions were strictly forbidden from passing the road in front of the shrine; even though it was the main road to Tōrin-ji, funeral routes to that temple used detours. === Rituals === === Shinto rites === Until early modern times, festival day was September 19; after calendar reform it changed to October 19, and now the annual festival is October 15. At the festival, "Oka Kagura," {{ill|lt=Sanbasō|Q11356796|WD=Q11356796}}, and Torisashi dance are dedicated at the precinct chōya building. "Oka Kagura" was originally a {{ill|lt=lion dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866}} performed on the first horse day of FebruaryTateo Hamano, 'Itō-shi,' written in {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era}} (mid-19th century)., thought to be a rite related to Inari. After a period of interruption, it was revived in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 59 and has been dedicated at the annual festival. Torisashi dance is also dedicated at other city shrines; each takes motifs from '{{ill|lt=Soga Monogatari|Q217886|WD=Q217886}}' and depicts the Soga brothers, disguised as bird catchers, avenging their father. Sanbasō is also widely distributed as ritual performance across the {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822}}. === Priests === In early modern times, Tōrin-ji managed the shrine as bettōThe temple was a Shugendō temple of the {{ill|lt=Tōzan-ha|Q11487826|WD=Q11487826}} since the {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853}} era (first half of 16th c.); now {{ill|lt=Sōtō school|Q749085|WD=Q749085}} ('Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku')., but at the start of Meiji, with {{ill|lt=shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, its monk returned to lay status, took the surname Asahi, and the Asahi family has continued the office. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Kuzumi-jinja no Ōkusu 20100612.jpg|thumb|150px|Hōsō deity and giant camphor]] The honden is a 2-ken-square {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545}} structure protected by a covering building ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ooiya|1=UNKNOWN}}), rebuilt in Genroku 10. Its transom has carvings of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=foxes|1=UNKNOWN}} and other motifs. The covering structure dates from the {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō period}} (early 19th c.). The haiden is a steel-reinforced concrete {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575}} tiled building, 3 bays by 2 bays, built in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 40. === Precinct shrines === Kumano Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, Mishima Shrine, Hachiman Shrine (merging Shinmei and Kasuga shrines), and Hōsō deity shrine. Hōsō deity is enshrined in a stone hokora under the giant camphor natural monument; because some gazetteers call it "Inari," it is thought Inari was worshiped here as {{ill|lt=hōsō deity|Q7543689|WD=Q7543689}}. === Cultural property === *{{ill|lt=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine|Q11621016|WD=Q11621016}} - a giant camphor north (left) of the haiden, trunk circumference about 15 m at chest height. Said to be over 1,000 years oldAlso described as reaching 2,000 years as of {{ill|lt=1987|Q2422|WD=Q2422}} in 'Nihon no Kamigami'.; designated national natural monument on February 28, {{ill|lt=1933|Q18726|WD=Q18726}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 8). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, Kōgakkan University Press, 1981 * Tanigawa Ken'ichi (ed.), 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places' vol.10 Tōkai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くすみしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧郷社]] [[Category:伊東市の歴史]] [[Category:伊東市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

Kuzumi Shrine |画像 = [[File:Kuzumi-jinja 20100612.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|34|57|50.6|N|139|05|56|E}}}} |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163|1=Itō, Shizuoka}}1-16-40 Babachō | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 57 | 緯度秒 = 50.6 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 5 | 経度秒 = 56 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | ISO = JP-22 | 地図 = Japan Shizuoka | ラベル位置 = left |祭神 = Kuzumi deity |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |別名 = Oka no Gōsha |札所等 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = Oka Kagura / Torisashi Odori ({{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}) }} '''Kuzumi Shrine''' (くずみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163|1=Itō, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is identified with "Kuzumi (Kutsumi) Shrine" of Tagata District, {{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|WD=Q860928|1=Izu Province}}, in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} list; its former rank was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It stands at the northern foot of a small hill about 30 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|1=Metre}} high in the southern urban area of Itō. In the densely wooded precinct rises a giant {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=camphor tree|1=UNKNOWN}} designated a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}. === Shrine name === A {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834|1=Munafuda}} of the present honden shows that in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1697|Q7702|WD=Q7702|1=1697}}) it was called "Kuzumi Taisha." Earlier names include "Kusumi Shrine" and "Kugo Taisha," and after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} it was renamed to the current form'Shizuoka-ken Jinja-shi,' Shizuoka-ken Chōdo Kenkyūkai, 1941.. In this case, the shrine name is derived from local place names (ancient {{ill|lt=Kusumi-gō (Itō)|Q721163|WD=Q721163|lt=Kusumi-gō|1=Itō, Shizuoka}}, medieval Kusumi / Kuzumi / Nanmi-shō). It is thought the old reading "kutsumi" voiced to "kuzumi" and became current "kuzumi"'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.'. The site belonged to Oka village until early modern times, and up to {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 47 ({{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476|1=1972}}) the address was Ito-shi Oka Aza Miyanoue, so it is still commonly called "Oka no Gōsha." === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is '''Kuzumi deity''', with {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}) in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. The principal deity is variously said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}} or a related clan deity, or medicinal deities such as {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}, but none is certain. Since sites in this area date back to the {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}}, it is considered to have been inhabited early and the deity likely revered from old times as the tutelary deity of the area. The aiden deity was invited later by the {{ill|lt=Itō clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280|1=Itō clan}}; because their ancestor {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketaka|Q24860857|WD=Q24860857|lt=Kudō Suketaka (Kudō Ietsugu / Itō Ietsugu)|1=Kudō Suketaka}} revered Inari, it is thought Inari was merged here'Nihon no Kamigami.'. The Itō lineage is said to have followed Suketaka in revering Inari, and among branches dispersed to various regions there were many cases of inviting Inari at their new settlements. === Origin and history === From ancient times, rites were likely performed to the sacred grove where divine spirit dwelled, still flourishing in the precinct today. The "Kuzumi Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' is considered this shrineThere is also a theory identifying it with {{ill|lt=Yumae Shrine|Q11563474|WD=Q11563474|1=Yuzen Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}} (e.g., 'Nihon Chiri Shiryō')., but its name does not appear in the '{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkaichō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkaichō}},' a {{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}. In the medieval period it received reverence and protection from the Itō clan based in this area. Itō Suketaka repaired the shrine and founded nearby Tōrin-ji as a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} to manage it'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.' Tōrin-ji's mountain name is "Inari-zan," thought to derive from enshrining Inari ('Nihon no Kamigami'). There is also a tradition that it was founded by Sukechika (Suketaka's grandson) to pray for the soul of his son {{ill|lt=Kawazu Sukeyasu|Q11553998|WD=Q11553998|lt=Sukeyasu|1=Kawazu Sukeyasu}}, slain by {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketsune|Q11479615|WD=Q11479615|1=Kudō Suketsune}}, his in-law cousin (same source). See also "{{ill|lt=The Soga Brothers' Revenge|Q10324810|WD=Q10324810|1=Revenge of the Soga Brothers}}.". Though Itō power declined after death of Suketaka's grandson {{ill|lt=Itō Sukechika|Q11379553|WD=Q11379553|lt=Sukechika|1=Itō Sukechika}}, descendants remaining nearby appear to have continued faith in the shrine. According to shrine munafuda, in {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 15 ({{ill|lt=1610|Q6765|WD=Q6765|1=1610}}), {{ill|lt=Itō Masayo|Q9385264|WD=Q9385264|lt=Itō Masa(yo)yo|1=Itō Masayo}} rebuilt after fire (though this is said to refer to the stone hokora for Inari/Hōsō deity under the great camphor noted belowAkiyama Akira, 'Tōshū Shikō,' written in {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} 12 (1800).). Until {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, local rulers periodically donated rice offerings. In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) April, it was ranked gōsha. The shrine was also treated as sacred by nearby residents. It is said funeral processions were strictly forbidden from passing the road in front of the shrine; even though it was the main road to Tōrin-ji, funeral routes to that temple used detours. === Rituals === === Shinto rites === Until early modern times, festival day was September 19; after calendar reform it changed to October 19, and now the annual festival is October 15. At the festival, "Oka Kagura," {{ill|lt=Sanbasō|Q11356796|WD=Q11356796|1=Sanbasō}}, and Torisashi dance are dedicated at the precinct chōya building. "Oka Kagura" was originally a {{ill|lt=lion dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866|1=Lion dance}} performed on the first horse day of FebruaryTateo Hamano, 'Itō-shi,' written in {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era|1=Kaei}} (mid-19th century)., thought to be a rite related to Inari. After a period of interruption, it was revived in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 59 and has been dedicated at the annual festival. Torisashi dance is also dedicated at other city shrines; each takes motifs from '{{ill|lt=Soga Monogatari|Q217886|WD=Q217886|1=Soga Monogatari}}' and depicts the Soga brothers, disguised as bird catchers, avenging their father. Sanbasō is also widely distributed as ritual performance across the {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822|1=Izu Peninsula}}. === Priests === In early modern times, Tōrin-ji managed the shrine as bettōThe temple was a Shugendō temple of the {{ill|lt=Tōzan-ha|Q11487826|WD=Q11487826|1=Tōzan-ha}} since the {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|1=Tenbun}} era (first half of 16th c.); now {{ill|lt=Sōtō school|Q749085|WD=Q749085|1=Sōtō}} ('Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku')., but at the start of Meiji, with {{ill|lt=shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, its monk returned to lay status, took the surname Asahi, and the Asahi family has continued the office. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Kuzumi-jinja no Ōkusu 20100612.jpg|thumb|150px|Hōsō deity and giant camphor]] The honden is a 2-ken-square {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} structure protected by a covering building ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ooiya|1=UNKNOWN}}), rebuilt in Genroku 10. Its transom has carvings of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=foxes|1=UNKNOWN}} and other motifs. The covering structure dates from the {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō period|1=Tenpō}} (early 19th c.). The haiden is a steel-reinforced concrete {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} tiled building, 3 bays by 2 bays, built in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40. === Precinct shrines === Kumano Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, Mishima Shrine, Hachiman Shrine (merging Shinmei and Kasuga shrines), and Hōsō deity shrine. Hōsō deity is enshrined in a stone hokora under the giant camphor natural monument; because some gazetteers call it "Inari," it is thought Inari was worshiped here as {{ill|lt=hōsō deity|Q7543689|WD=Q7543689|1=Smallpox demon}}. === Cultural property === *{{ill|lt=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine|Q11621016|WD=Q11621016|1=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine}} - a giant camphor north (left) of the haiden, trunk circumference about 15 m at chest height. Said to be over 1,000 years oldAlso described as reaching 2,000 years as of {{ill|lt=1987|Q2422|WD=Q2422|1=March 18}} in 'Nihon no Kamigami'.; designated national natural monument on February 28, {{ill|lt=1933|Q18726|WD=Q18726|1=1933}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 8). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, Kōgakkan University Press, 1981 * Tanigawa Ken'ichi (ed.), 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places' vol.10 Tōkai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くすみしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧郷社]] [[Category:伊東市の歴史]] [[Category:伊東市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

== street address (P6375) == * {'text': '静岡県伊東市馬場町1-16-40', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Kuzumi-jinja}} [[JA:葛見神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11621015}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Kuzumi Shrine |画像 = [[File:Kuzumi-jinja 20100612.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|34|57|50.6|N|139|05|56|E}}}} |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163|1=Itō, Shizuoka}}1-16-40 Babachō | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 57 | 緯度秒 = 50.6 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 5 | 経度秒 = 56 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | ISO = JP-22 | 地図 = Japan Shizuoka | ラベル位置 = left |祭神 = Kuzumi deity |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |別名 = Oka no Gōsha |札所等 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = Oka Kagura / Torisashi Odori ({{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}) }} '''Kuzumi Shrine''' (くずみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163|1=Itō, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is identified with "Kuzumi (Kutsumi) Shrine" of Tagata District, {{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|WD=Q860928|1=Izu Province}}, in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} list; its former rank was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It stands at the northern foot of a small hill about 30 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|1=Metre}} high in the southern urban area of Itō. In the densely wooded precinct rises a giant {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=camphor tree|1=UNKNOWN}} designated a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}. === Shrine name === A {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834|1=Munafuda}} of the present honden shows that in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1697|Q7702|WD=Q7702|1=1697}}) it was called "Kuzumi Taisha." Earlier names include "Kusumi Shrine" and "Kugo Taisha," and after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} it was renamed to the current form'Shizuoka-ken Jinja-shi,' Shizuoka-ken Chōdo Kenkyūkai, 1941.. In this case, the shrine name is derived from local place names (ancient {{ill|lt=Kusumi-gō (Itō)|Q721163|WD=Q721163|lt=Kusumi-gō|1=Itō, Shizuoka}}, medieval Kusumi / Kuzumi / Nanmi-shō). It is thought the old reading "kutsumi" voiced to "kuzumi" and became current "kuzumi"'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.'. The site belonged to Oka village until early modern times, and up to {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 47 ({{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476|1=1972}}) the address was Ito-shi Oka Aza Miyanoue, so it is still commonly called "Oka no Gōsha." === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is '''Kuzumi deity''', with {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}) in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. The principal deity is variously said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}} or a related clan deity, or medicinal deities such as {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}, but none is certain. Since sites in this area date back to the {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}}, it is considered to have been inhabited early and the deity likely revered from old times as the tutelary deity of the area. The aiden deity was invited later by the {{ill|lt=Itō clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280|1=Itō clan}}; because their ancestor {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketaka|Q24860857|WD=Q24860857|lt=Kudō Suketaka (Kudō Ietsugu / Itō Ietsugu)|1=Kudō Suketaka}} revered Inari, it is thought Inari was merged here'Nihon no Kamigami.'. The Itō lineage is said to have followed Suketaka in revering Inari, and among branches dispersed to various regions there were many cases of inviting Inari at their new settlements. === Origin and history === From ancient times, rites were likely performed to the sacred grove where divine spirit dwelled, still flourishing in the precinct today. The "Kuzumi Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' is considered this shrineThere is also a theory identifying it with {{ill|lt=Yumae Shrine|Q11563474|WD=Q11563474|1=Yuzen Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}} (e.g., 'Nihon Chiri Shiryō')., but its name does not appear in the '{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkaichō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkaichō}},' a {{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}. In the medieval period it received reverence and protection from the Itō clan based in this area. Itō Suketaka repaired the shrine and founded nearby Tōrin-ji as a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} to manage it'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.' Tōrin-ji's mountain name is "Inari-zan," thought to derive from enshrining Inari ('Nihon no Kamigami'). There is also a tradition that it was founded by Sukechika (Suketaka's grandson) to pray for the soul of his son {{ill|lt=Kawazu Sukeyasu|Q11553998|WD=Q11553998|lt=Sukeyasu|1=Kawazu Sukeyasu}}, slain by {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketsune|Q11479615|WD=Q11479615|1=Kudō Suketsune}}, his in-law cousin (same source). See also "{{ill|lt=The Soga Brothers' Revenge|Q10324810|WD=Q10324810|1=Revenge of the Soga Brothers}}.". Though Itō power declined after death of Suketaka's grandson {{ill|lt=Itō Sukechika|Q11379553|WD=Q11379553|lt=Sukechika|1=Itō Sukechika}}, descendants remaining nearby appear to have continued faith in the shrine. According to shrine munafuda, in {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 15 ({{ill|lt=1610|Q6765|WD=Q6765|1=1610}}), {{ill|lt=Itō Masayo|Q9385264|WD=Q9385264|lt=Itō Masa(yo)yo|1=Itō Masayo}} rebuilt after fire (though this is said to refer to the stone hokora for Inari/Hōsō deity under the great camphor noted belowAkiyama Akira, 'Tōshū Shikō,' written in {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} 12 (1800).). Until {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, local rulers periodically donated rice offerings. In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) April, it was ranked gōsha. The shrine was also treated as sacred by nearby residents. It is said funeral processions were strictly forbidden from passing the road in front of the shrine; even though it was the main road to Tōrin-ji, funeral routes to that temple used detours. === Rituals === === Shinto rites === Until early modern times, festival day was September 19; after calendar reform it changed to October 19, and now the annual festival is October 15. At the festival, "Oka Kagura," {{ill|lt=Sanbasō|Q11356796|WD=Q11356796|1=Sanbasō}}, and Torisashi dance are dedicated at the precinct chōya building. "Oka Kagura" was originally a {{ill|lt=lion dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866|1=Lion dance}} performed on the first horse day of FebruaryTateo Hamano, 'Itō-shi,' written in {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era|1=Kaei}} (mid-19th century)., thought to be a rite related to Inari. After a period of interruption, it was revived in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 59 and has been dedicated at the annual festival. Torisashi dance is also dedicated at other city shrines; each takes motifs from '{{ill|lt=Soga Monogatari|Q217886|WD=Q217886|1=Soga Monogatari}}' and depicts the Soga brothers, disguised as bird catchers, avenging their father. Sanbasō is also widely distributed as ritual performance across the {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822|1=Izu Peninsula}}. === Priests === In early modern times, Tōrin-ji managed the shrine as bettōThe temple was a Shugendō temple of the {{ill|lt=Tōzan-ha|Q11487826|WD=Q11487826|1=Tōzan-ha}} since the {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|1=Tenbun}} era (first half of 16th c.); now {{ill|lt=Sōtō school|Q749085|WD=Q749085|1=Sōtō}} ('Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku')., but at the start of Meiji, with {{ill|lt=shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, its monk returned to lay status, took the surname Asahi, and the Asahi family has continued the office. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Kuzumi-jinja no Ōkusu 20100612.jpg|thumb|150px|Hōsō deity and giant camphor]] The honden is a 2-ken-square {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} structure protected by a covering building ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ooiya|1=UNKNOWN}}), rebuilt in Genroku 10. Its transom has carvings of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=foxes|1=UNKNOWN}} and other motifs. The covering structure dates from the {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō period|1=Tenpō}} (early 19th c.). The haiden is a steel-reinforced concrete {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} tiled building, 3 bays by 2 bays, built in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40. === Precinct shrines === Kumano Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, Mishima Shrine, Hachiman Shrine (merging Shinmei and Kasuga shrines), and Hōsō deity shrine. Hōsō deity is enshrined in a stone hokora under the giant camphor natural monument; because some gazetteers call it "Inari," it is thought Inari was worshiped here as {{ill|lt=hōsō deity|Q7543689|WD=Q7543689|1=Smallpox demon}}. === Cultural property === *{{ill|lt=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine|Q11621016|WD=Q11621016|1=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine}} - a giant camphor north (left) of the haiden, trunk circumference about 15 m at chest height. Said to be over 1,000 years oldAlso described as reaching 2,000 years as of {{ill|lt=1987|Q2422|WD=Q2422|1=March 18}} in 'Nihon no Kamigami'.; designated national natural monument on February 28, {{ill|lt=1933|Q18726|WD=Q18726|1=1933}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 8). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, Kōgakkan University Press, 1981 * Tanigawa Ken'ichi (ed.), 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places' vol.10 Tōkai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くすみしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧郷社]] [[Category:伊東市の歴史]] [[Category:伊東市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qqq]]

It is thought the old reading "kutsumi" voiced to "kuzumi" and became current "kuzumi"'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.'. The site belonged to Oka village until early modern times, and up to {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 47 ({{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476|1=1972}}) the address was Ito-shi Oka Aza Miyanoue, so it is still commonly called "Oka no Gōsha." === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is '''Kuzumi deity''', with {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}) in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. The principal deity is variously said to be {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611}} or a related clan deity, or medicinal deities such as {{ill|Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōnamuchi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona|WD=Q3275687}}, but none is certain. Since sites in this area date back to the {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|WD=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}}, it is considered to have been inhabited early and the deity likely revered from old times as the tutelary deity of the area. The aiden deity was invited later by the {{ill|lt=Itō clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280|1=Itō clan}}; because their ancestor {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketaka|Q24860857|WD=Q24860857|lt=Kudō Suketaka (Kudō Ietsugu / Itō Ietsugu)|1=Kudō Suketaka}} revered Inari, it is thought Inari was merged here'Nihon no Kamigami.'. The Itō lineage is said to have followed Suketaka in revering Inari, and among branches dispersed to various regions there were many cases of inviting Inari at their new settlements. === Origin and history === From ancient times, rites were likely performed to the sacred grove where divine spirit dwelled, still flourishing in the precinct today. The "Kuzumi Shrine" in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' is considered this shrineThere is also a theory identifying it with {{ill|lt=Yumae Shrine|Q11563474|WD=Q11563474|1=Yuzen Shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}} (e.g., 'Nihon Chiri Shiryō')., but its name does not appear in the '{{ill|lt=Izu no Kuni Shinkaichō|Q17193087|WD=Q17193087|1=Izu no Kuni Shinkaichō}},' a {{ill|lt=Kokunai Jinmyōchō|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|1=Izumi Province Divine Name Register}}. In the medieval period it received reverence and protection from the Itō clan based in this area. Itō Suketaka repaired the shrine and founded nearby Tōrin-ji as a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} to manage it'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.' Tōrin-ji's mountain name is "Inari-zan," thought to derive from enshrining Inari ('Nihon no Kamigami'). There is also a tradition that it was founded by Sukechika (Suketaka's grandson) to pray for the soul of his son {{ill|lt=Kawazu Sukeyasu|Q11553998|WD=Q11553998|lt=Sukeyasu|1=Kawazu Sukeyasu}}, slain by {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketsune|Q11479615|WD=Q11479615|1=Kudō Suketsune}}, his in-law cousin (same source). See also "{{ill|lt=The Soga Brothers' Revenge|Q10324810|WD=Q10324810|1=Revenge of the Soga Brothers}}.". Though Itō power declined after death of Suketaka's grandson {{ill|lt=Itō Sukechika|Q11379553|WD=Q11379553|lt=Sukechika|1=Itō Sukechika}}, descendants remaining nearby appear to have continued faith in the shrine. According to shrine munafuda, in {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|WD=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 15 ({{ill|lt=1610|Q6765|WD=Q6765|1=1610}}), {{ill|lt=Itō Masayo|Q9385264|WD=Q9385264|lt=Itō Masa(yo)yo|1=Itō Masayo}} rebuilt after fire (though this is said to refer to the stone hokora for Inari/Hōsō deity under the great camphor noted belowAkiyama Akira, 'Tōshū Shikō,' written in {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} 12 (1800).). Until {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, local rulers periodically donated rice offerings. In Meiji 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) April, it was ranked gōsha. The shrine was also treated as sacred by nearby residents. It is said funeral processions were strictly forbidden from passing the road in front of the shrine; even though it was the main road to Tōrin-ji, funeral routes to that temple used detours. === Rituals === === Shinto rites === Until early modern times, festival day was September 19; after calendar reform it changed to October 19, and now the annual festival is October 15. At the festival, "Oka Kagura," {{ill|lt=Sanbasō|Q11356796|WD=Q11356796|1=Sanbasō}}, and Torisashi dance are dedicated at the precinct chōya building. "Oka Kagura" was originally a {{ill|lt=lion dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866|1=Lion dance}} performed on the first horse day of FebruaryTateo Hamano, 'Itō-shi,' written in {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era|1=Kaei}} (mid-19th century)., thought to be a rite related to Inari. After a period of interruption, it was revived in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 59 and has been dedicated at the annual festival. Torisashi dance is also dedicated at other city shrines; each takes motifs from '{{ill|lt=Soga Monogatari|Q217886|WD=Q217886|1=Soga Monogatari}}' and depicts the Soga brothers, disguised as bird catchers, avenging their father. Sanbasō is also widely distributed as ritual performance across the {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822|1=Izu Peninsula}}. === Priests === In early modern times, Tōrin-ji managed the shrine as bettōThe temple was a Shugendō temple of the {{ill|lt=Tōzan-ha|Q11487826|WD=Q11487826|1=Tōzan-ha}} since the {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|1=Tenbun}} era (first half of 16th c.); now {{ill|lt=Sōtō school|Q749085|WD=Q749085|1=Sōtō}} ('Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku')., but at the start of Meiji, with {{ill|lt=shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, its monk returned to lay status, took the surname Asahi, and the Asahi family has continued the office. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Kuzumi-jinja no Ōkusu 20100612.jpg|thumb|150px|Hōsō deity and giant camphor]] The honden is a 2-ken-square {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} structure protected by a covering building ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ooiya|1=UNKNOWN}}), rebuilt in Genroku 10. Its transom has carvings of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=foxes|1=UNKNOWN}} and other motifs. The covering structure dates from the {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō period|1=Tenpō}} (early 19th c.). The haiden is a steel-reinforced concrete {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} tiled building, 3 bays by 2 bays, built in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40. === Precinct shrines === Kumano Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, Mishima Shrine, Hachiman Shrine (merging Shinmei and Kasuga shrines), and Hōsō deity shrine. Hōsō deity is enshrined in a stone hokora under the giant camphor natural monument; because some gazetteers call it "Inari," it is thought Inari was worshiped here as {{ill|lt=hōsō deity|Q7543689|WD=Q7543689|1=Smallpox demon}}. === Cultural property === *{{ill|lt=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine|Q11621016|WD=Q11621016|1=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine}} - a giant camphor north (left) of the haiden, trunk circumference about 15 m at chest height. Said to be over 1,000 years oldAlso described as reaching 2,000 years as of {{ill|lt=1987|Q2422|WD=Q2422|1=March 18}} in 'Nihon no Kamigami'.; designated national natural monument on February 28, {{ill|lt=1933|Q18726|WD=Q18726|1=1933}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 8). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, Kōgakkan University Press, 1981 * Tanigawa Ken'ichi (ed.), 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places' vol.10 Tōkai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くすみしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧郷社]] [[Category:伊東市の歴史]] [[Category:伊東市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|34|57|50.6|N|139|05|56|E}}}} |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163|1=Itō, Shizuoka}}1-16-40 Babachō | 緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 57 | 緯度秒 = 50.6 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 5 | 経度秒 = 56 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | ISO = JP-22 | 地図 = Japan Shizuoka | ラベル位置 = left |祭神 = Kuzumi deity |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} |別名 = Oka no Gōsha |札所等 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = Oka Kagura / Torisashi Odori ({{ill|lt=October 15|Q2919|WD=Q2919|1=October 15}}) }} '''Kuzumi Shrine''' (くずみじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Itō|Q721163|WD=Q721163|1=Itō, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is identified with "Kuzumi (Kutsumi) Shrine" of Tagata District, {{ill|lt=Izu Province|Q860928|WD=Q860928|1=Izu Province}}, in the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} list; its former rank was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. It stands at the northern foot of a small hill about 30 {{ill|lt=meters|Q11573|WD=Q11573|1=Metre}} high in the southern urban area of Itō. In the densely wooded precinct rises a giant {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=camphor tree|1=UNKNOWN}} designated a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790|1=Natural monument}}. === Shrine name === A {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834|1=Munafuda}} of the present honden shows that in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1697|Q7702|WD=Q7702|1=1697}}) it was called "Kuzumi Taisha." Earlier names include "Kusumi Shrine" and "Kugo Taisha," and after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707|1=Meiji Restoration}} it was renamed to the current form'Shizuoka-ken Jinja-shi,' Shizuoka-ken Chōdo Kenkyūkai, 1941.. In this case, the shrine name is derived from local place names (ancient {{ill|lt=Kusumi-gō (Itō)|Q721163|WD=Q721163|lt=Kusumi-gō|1=Itō, Shizuoka}}, medieval Kusumi / Kuzumi / Nanmi-shō). It is thought the old reading "kutsumi" voiced to "kuzumi" and became current "kuzumi"'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku.'. The site belonged to Oka village until early modern times, and up to {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 47 ({{ill|lt=1972|Q2476|WD=Q2476|1=1972}}) the address was Ito-shi Oka Aza Miyanoue, so it is still commonly called "Oka no Gōsha." === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is '''Kuzumi deity''', with {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Ukanomitama}} ({{ill|lt=Inari deity|Q719665|WD=Q719665|1=Inari Ōkami}}) in the {{ill|lt=aiden|Q135009883|WD=Q135009883|1=Aidono}}. The aiden deity was invited later by the {{ill|lt=Itō clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280|1=Itō clan}}; because their ancestor {{ill|lt=Kudō Suketaka|Q24860857|WD=Q24860857|lt=Kudō Suketaka (Kudō Ietsugu / Itō Ietsugu)|1=Kudō Suketaka}} revered Inari, it is thought Inari was merged here'Nihon no Kamigami.'. See also "{{ill|lt=The Soga Brothers' Revenge|Q10324810|WD=Q10324810|1=Revenge of the Soga Brothers}}.". It is said funeral processions were strictly forbidden from passing the road in front of the shrine; even though it was the main road to Tōrin-ji, funeral routes to that temple used detours. === Rituals === === Shinto rites === Until early modern times, festival day was September 19; after calendar reform it changed to October 19, and now the annual festival is October 15. At the festival, "Oka Kagura," {{ill|lt=Sanbasō|Q11356796|WD=Q11356796|1=Sanbasō}}, and Torisashi dance are dedicated at the precinct chōya building. "Oka Kagura" was originally a {{ill|lt=lion dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866|1=Lion dance}} performed on the first horse day of FebruaryTateo Hamano, 'Itō-shi,' written in {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era|1=Kaei}} (mid-19th century)., thought to be a rite related to Inari. After a period of interruption, it was revived in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 59 and has been dedicated at the annual festival. Torisashi dance is also dedicated at other city shrines; each takes motifs from '{{ill|lt=Soga Monogatari|Q217886|WD=Q217886|1=Soga Monogatari}}' and depicts the Soga brothers, disguised as bird catchers, avenging their father. Sanbasō is also widely distributed as ritual performance across the {{ill|lt=Izu Peninsula|Q822822|WD=Q822822|1=Izu Peninsula}}. === Priests === In early modern times, Tōrin-ji managed the shrine as bettōThe temple was a Shugendō temple of the {{ill|lt=Tōzan-ha|Q11487826|WD=Q11487826|1=Tōzan-ha}} since the {{ill|lt=Tenbun|Q1192853|WD=Q1192853|1=Tenbun}} era (first half of 16th c.); now {{ill|lt=Sōtō school|Q749085|WD=Q749085|1=Sōtō}} ('Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku')., but at the start of Meiji, with {{ill|lt=shinbutsu bunri|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, its monk returned to lay status, took the surname Asahi, and the Asahi family has continued the office. === Shrine buildings === [[File:Kuzumi-jinja no Ōkusu 20100612.jpg|thumb|150px|Hōsō deity and giant camphor]] The honden is a 2-ken-square {{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|WD=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}} structure protected by a covering building ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ooiya|1=UNKNOWN}}), rebuilt in Genroku 10. Its transom has carvings of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=foxes|1=UNKNOWN}} and other motifs. The covering structure dates from the {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō period|1=Tenpō}} (early 19th c.). The haiden is a steel-reinforced concrete {{ill|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|Q13308575|WD=Q13308575|1=Kirizuma-style}} tiled building, 3 bays by 2 bays, built in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 40. === Precinct shrines === Kumano Shrine, Hakusan Shrine, Mishima Shrine, Hachiman Shrine (merging Shinmei and Kasuga shrines), and Hōsō deity shrine. Hōsō deity is enshrined in a stone hokora under the giant camphor natural monument; because some gazetteers call it "Inari," it is thought Inari was worshiped here as {{ill|lt=hōsō deity|Q7543689|WD=Q7543689|1=Smallpox demon}}. === Cultural property === *{{ill|lt=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine|Q11621016|WD=Q11621016|1=Great Camphor of Kuzumi Shrine}} - a giant camphor north (left) of the haiden, trunk circumference about 15 m at chest height. Said to be over 1,000 years oldAlso described as reaching 2,000 years as of {{ill|lt=1987|Q2422|WD=Q2422|1=March 18}} in 'Nihon no Kamigami'.; designated national natural monument on February 28, {{ill|lt=1933|Q18726|WD=Q18726|1=1933}} ({{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 8). === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * 'Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku' vol.10 Tōkaidō 5, Kōgakkan University Press, 1981 * Tanigawa Ken'ichi (ed.), 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places' vol.10 Tōkai, Hakusuisha, 1987 ISBN 4-560-02220-8 {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kuzumi Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧郷社]] [[Category:伊東市の歴史]] [[Category:伊東市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]
